content.text: The gender division is a hierarchical social division seen everywhere, but rarely recognized in the study of politics. It is often understood as natural and unchangeable, but it is actually based on social expectations and stereotypes rather than biology.

content.title: Sexual Division of Labour
content.text: A system in which all work inside the home is either done by the women of the family or organized by them through domestic helpers. While women do housework, men are expected to do work outside the home. This division makes women's work unpaid and invisible.

Women in different parts of the world organized and agitated for equal rights, including voting rights, better educational opportunities, and career prospects. Radical movements also aimed at equality in personal and family life.

content.title: Feminist
content.text: A woman or a man who believes in equal rights and opportunities for women and men.
Ours is still a male-dominated, patriarchal society. Women face disadvantage, discrimination, and oppression in various ways:

content.title: Aspects of Discrimination
items: Literacy Rate: Only 54% among women compared to 76% among men (2011 Census). Parents often prefer spending resources on boys' education.
Unpaid Work: An average Indian woman works one hour more than an average man every day, yet much of her work is not valued.
Wages: The Equal Remuneration Act, 1976 provides for equal wages, but women are still paid less in sports, cinema, and factories.
Child Sex Ratio: Sex-selective abortion has led to a decline in child sex ratio (919 girls per 1000 boys in 2011).
Violence: Reports of harassment, exploitation, and domestic violence against women are widespread in both urban and rural areas.

content.text: Issues related to women's well-being often go ignored unless women control power. In India, women's representation in the Lok Sabha reached 14.36% for the first time in 2019, while state assemblies remain below 5%.

content.title: Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (2023)
content.text: The Women’s Reservation Act, 2023 provides for 33% reservation of seats for women in the Lok Sabha, State Legislative Assemblies, and the Delhi Assembly. This follows the successful model of Panchayati Raj, where one-third of seats in local bodies have been reserved for women for decades.
Religious differences are often expressed in politics, but they are not always dangerous if handled equally. Key perspectives include:

content.title: Diverse Views
items: Gandhiji: Believed religion can never be separated from politics, referring to moral values (ethics) drawn from religion rather than any particular creed.
Human Rights Groups: Demand special steps to protect religious minorities from communal riots.
Women's Movement: Argues that family laws of all religions discriminate against women and must be made more equitable.

content.text: Communalism is a situation where religion is seen as the principal basis of social community. It involves thinking that followers of one religion have the same interests and cannot live as equal citizens with others.

content.title: Forms of Communalism in Politics
items: Everyday Beliefs: Religious prejudices, stereotypes, and belief in the superiority of one's religion.
Political Dominance: A quest for majoritarian dominance or a desire for a separate political unit among minorities.
Electoral Mobilisation: Use of sacred symbols, religious leaders, and emotional appeals to bring followers together.
Communal Violence: Riots and massacres, as seen during the Partition of India and Pakistan.

content.text: The makers of the Indian Constitution chose a secular model to combat communalism. Secularism is a foundation of our country, not just an ideology.

content.title: Constitutional Provisions for Secularism
items: No Official Religion: India does not give special status to any religion (unlike Buddhism in Sri Lanka or Islam in Pakistan).
Freedom of Religion: All individuals have the freedom to profess, practice, and propagate any religion.
Non-discrimination: Prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion.
State Intervention: Allows the state to intervene (e.g., banning untouchability) to ensure equality within religious communities.

content.text: Caste division is unique to India. It is an extreme form of hereditary occupational division sanctioned by rituals. It involves endogamy (marrying within the caste) and historically, the practice of untouchability.

content.title: Social Reformers
content.text: Leaders like Jotiba Phule, Gandhiji, B.R. Ambedkar, and Periyar Ramaswami Naicker worked to establish a society without caste inequalities.

Notions of caste hierarchy are breaking down due to socio-economic changes such as urbanisation, growth of literacy, occupational mobility, and land reforms. However, caste has not disappeared; endogability persists, and the effects of past advantages/disadvantages continue to influence economic status today.
Casteism in politics is based on the flawed belief that caste is the sole basis of social community. It manifests in various ways:

content.title: Political Manifestations
items: Candidate Selection: Parties choose candidates based on the caste composition of the electorate.
Castes as Vote Banks: Parties make appeals to caste sentiments to muster support.
Political Consciousness: Universal adult franchise gave new power to castes previously treated as inferior.

content.title: Limits of Caste in Elections
content.text: Caste alone cannot determine election results because: (1) No constituency has a clear majority of one caste, (2) No party wins all votes of a caste, and (3) Performance of the government and popularity of leaders also matter.
Politics also influences caste by bringing identities into the political arena. It politicises caste through:

content.title: Politicisation Process
items: Expanding Identity: Caste groups try to become bigger by incorporating sub-castes.
Coalitions: Different caste groups enter into dialogues and negotiations.
New Groups: Formation of 'backward' and 'forward' caste groups.

content.title: Dual Nature of Caste Politics
content.text: While it can give disadvantaged communities space to demand their share of power and dignity, exclusive attention to caste can divert attention from poverty and development, leading to tension and violence.

glossary.definition: A system where all work inside the home is done or organized by women, while men work outside.
A person (man or woman) who believes in equal rights and opportunities for women and men.
Literally 'rule by father', a social system that values men more and gives them power over women.
The belief that religion is the principal basis of social community and identity.
A state that does not have an official religion and provides religious freedom to all citizens.
Laws that deal with family-related matters like marriage, divorce, and inheritance; in India, these vary by religion.
A ladder-like formation where caste groups are placed from the 'highest' to the 'lowest'.
The shift of population from rural areas to urban areas (cities).
The shift from one occupation to another, especially across generations.

summary_points: Gender division is a social hierarchy based on stereotypes rather than biology, often leading to a sexual division of labour.
Feminist movements seek to improve the status of women in both public life and personal family relations.
India remains a patriarchal society where women face discrimination in literacy, wages, and representation.
The 2023 Women's Reservation Act (33% seats) is a landmark step towards political empowerment of women.
Communalism poses a major threat to democracy by pitting religious groups against each other and seeking majoritarian dominance.
India's secular constitution provides religious freedom and prohibits state support for any particular religion.
Caste is unique to India and involves hereditary divisions that are slowly breaking down due to urbanisation and education.
While caste plays a significant role in political mobilisation, it is not the sole factor determining election outcomes.
Politics can both empower disadvantaged castes and cause social tension when focused exclusively on caste identities.
